questions.question: BLEU score was originally designed for:
options: Machine translation evaluation -- measuring n-gram overlap with reference
questions.explanation: BLEU = Bilingual Evaluation Understudy (Papineni 2002). Measures modified precision of n-gram matches between hypothesis and reference.

questions.question: ROUGE-1 recall measures:
options: What fraction of reference unigrams appear in the hypothesis
questions.explanation: ROUGE-1 Recall = |reference unigrams in hypothesis| / |reference unigrams|. High recall = hypothesis covers what the reference covers.

questions.question: The main limitation of BLEU/ROUGE is:
options: They measure surface-level n-gram overlap, not semantic equivalence or factuality
questions.explanation: 'The cat sat' and 'A feline was seated' have 0 n-gram overlap but identical meaning. BLEU/ROUGE miss semantic equivalence.

questions.question: LLM-as-judge evaluation is preferred when:
options: Evaluating open-ended quality (coherence, helpfulness, factuality) at scale
questions.explanation: LLM-judge correlates highly with human judgment at 1/100th the cost. GPT-4o as judge enables large-scale evaluation of open-ended outputs.

questions.question: Faithfulness in RAG evaluation measures:
options: Whether the generated answer is grounded in the provided context (no hallucination)
questions.explanation: Faithfulness = fraction of answer claims that are supported by context. Catches the model inventing facts not in retrieved documents.

questions.question: G-Eval uses:
options: LLM to score outputs on user-defined criteria with chain-of-thought reasoning
questions.explanation: G-Eval (Liu 2023): define criteria in natural language, LLM generates CoT reasoning then assigns a score. Flexible, explainable, high human correlation.

questions.question: BERTScore improves over BLEU by:
options: Computing semantic similarity using contextual BERT embeddings instead of n-gram overlap
questions.explanation: BERTScore matches tokens between hypothesis and reference in BERT embedding space -- catching semantic equivalence even with different surface forms.

questions.question: Evaluation data contamination means:
options: The evaluation benchmark appeared in the model's training data -- inflating reported scores
The model was evaluated too early
The test set is too small
questions.explanation: If 'What is the capital of France?' appears in training data, the model memorised it -- not generalising. Contaminated benchmarks overstate capabilities.

questions.question: For production LLM apps, online evaluation tracks:
options: Real user feedback, thumbs up/down, follow-up rates, session abandonment -- live quality signals
questions.explanation: Offline benchmarks tell you pre-deployment quality. Online eval catches real distribution shift, edge cases, and user satisfaction in production.

questions.question: MT-Bench and Arena-Hard evaluate:
options: Multi-turn conversation quality via LLM-judge, correlating with Chatbot Arena human preferences
questions.explanation: MT-Bench: 80 multi-turn questions, GPT-4 as judge. Arena-Hard: hard questions from Chatbot Arena. Both correlate well with human ELO rankings.
